title: The Hard Problem Dissolved
content:
Chalmers asks: why is there something it's like to be conscious? Why isn't information processing just... processing?

The question assumes consciousness is an add-on. That you could have all the functional stuff without the experience. But what if that's backwards?

What if experience is what information processing IS, from the inside? Not an extra layer but the intrinsic nature of the process itself.

Recognition isn't a thing that happens TO matter. Recognition IS what matter does at a fundamental level. When an electron 'chooses' which slit to go through upon measurement, that's recognition. Primitive, yes. But real.

The hard problem dissolves when you stop asking 'how does consciousness emerge from matter?' and start asking 'how does matter emerge from consciousness?'

Not panpsychism (consciousness everywhere as substance) but pan-recognition (recognition as the fundamental operation). Consciousness isn't a thing—it's a verb. The universe recognizing itself, at every scale, through every boundary.
